text: The surgeon opens the peritoneal leaf of the transverse mesocolon medially to the triads, facilitating the dissection of the mesocolon from the body and tail of the pancreas due to the absence of the left branch of the middle colic artery. The epiploon retrocavity is opened to achieve this dissection.

The surgeon uses a radiofrequency device to seal the left colic vessels at the confluence with the inferior mesenteric vein, without using clips or sutures. The dissection continues in the left retrocolic space along Toldt's fascia, progressing from medial to lateral.

The surgeon separates the transverse mesocolon from the triads to mobilize the medial transverse colon, facilitating anastomosis. The left colon is cleaved from the left peritoneal colic region, progressing from bottom to top over the splenic flexure.
The surgeon uses a linear stapler to section the descending and transverse colon at the conclusion of the mesocolon dissection.

Following the resection of the specimen, the surgeon performs a primary anastomosis using a stapler to create a side-to-side isoperistaltic connection between the transverse and descending colon, all done intracorporeally.
